data.title: Lemonade: The Future Of Insurance Is Still Trading At Early-Stage Levels
data.description:
To me, there has never been a better time to increase your exposure to small-cap stocks. Recent market volatility has caused smaller-cap, traditionally more macroeconomically sensitive stocks to underperform versus the titans of the S&P 500. That gap creates a great opening for long-term oriented investors to pick up shares of fantastic growth stocks at a discount.

One of the most unique plays that I favor is Lemonade (LMND), the tech-enabled insurance company that has recently expanded its home and renters' insurance portfolio to include car insurance (at a time that insurance fundamentals are very healthy in the auto industry, thanks to recent regulatory approvals for rate increases). Year to date, shares of Lemonade have lost ~15% of their value, creating a great opening for a new position.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Lemonade in February, prior to the tariffs-driven market crash and when Lemonade was still trading near $31 per share. Since then, Lemonade has continued to post accelerating premium growth, while loss ratios continue to trend downward. With Lemonade hitting all of the right fundamental notes and barely seeing any stock price appreciation, I'm reiterating my buy opinion on this name.

One single chart should serve to summarize the bull case for Lemonade, and that's shown below:

Over the past six quarters, Lemonade has shown accelerating trends in in-force premiums (the total recurring value of all of Lemonade's customer premiums, before the company cedes a portion to its reinsurers), while growing economies of scale have continued to push Lemonade's gross loss ratios downward (the company remarked that its most recent trailing twelve-month gross loss ratio of 73% was well within the company's tolerance levels, and that includes the one-time impact of LA wildfires).

A reduction in policy loss ratios is also joined by increasing economies of scale on the opex side, as Lemonade benefits in sales and marketing from greater scale as well as utilizing AI to speed up claims processing and removing manual labor. These moves are expected to help push Lemonade toward an adjusted EBITDA breakeven in FY26.

To me, Lemonade is rapidly becoming the gold standard for insurance of the future. It bypasses clunky marketplaces and agent-based sales processes to give quick quotes on a clean interface on its website, and its policy options now offer a wide spectrum of coverage ranging from home, renters, pet, car, and term life insurance (I have firsthand experience as a customer, with Lemonade as my home insurance provider).

I encourage investors to pick up this stock as it continues to gain momentum, while still being relatively unnoticed by the broader stock market.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Lemonade's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

As previously shown, Lemonade's in-force premium grew 27% y/y to $1.01 billion, accelerating one point versus 26% y/y growth in the preceding Q4. Meanwhile, revenue, which is Lemonade's cut of its premium stream after its reinsurance contracts, grew 27% y/y as well to $151.2 million, handily beating Wall Street's expectations of $145.0 million (+22% y/y) by a five-point margin.

One major driver of growth for Lemonade has been its recent push into car insurance. Management views a broad cross-selling opportunity available for Lemonade Car, as the company currently has over 2.5 million customers that can be offered a bundling opportunity. Cross-sales into existing Lemonade customers increased 100% y/y in Q1, and the company launched its car insurance in Colorado, the first of several planned new-state rollouts in 2025. Still today, Lemonade Car is only active in ~40% of the U.S. population, giving a wide breadth for continued expansion.

Lemonade's ability to continue growing premiums is a testament to the fact that its business is almost entirely macro-resistant. Even though consumers are pulling back on discretionary spending, Lemonade continues to see blazing growth trends. The only potential risk (that we have not yet seen materialize) is the possibility of continued weakness in home sales to slow down new home policy growth rates.

In keeping with the strength of its recent trends, Lemonade boosted its full-year revenue expectations to $661-$663 million (25-26% growth), versus a 24-25% range previously.

On the profitability front, we note that Lemonade's adjusted EBITDA declined to a -$47.0 million loss in the quarter. This was driven by a temporary spike in net loss ratios to 82%, up 4 points from 78% in the year-ago Q1:

The LA wildfires, in particular, contributed to a good chunk of this quarter's losses. In analyzing the impacts of the (hopefully) one-time event on Lemonade's results, the company noted that it suffered a $36 million gross loss (before reinsurance and other adjustments) and a $22 million underlying impact to adjusted EBITDA - without which the company's adjusted EBITDA losses would have improved on a year-over-year basis.

Risks and key takeaways

Amid this quarter's strength, we should proactively mention two risks that Lemonade is facing.

The first is that the company has noted a slight weakening in retention rates in its home policies. Per CFO Tim Bixby's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

Annual retention, or ADR, was 84%, a 4% decrease since this time last year, and down slightly versus 86% in the prior quarter. In broad strokes, we saw an unfavorable impact to ADR from our continuing effort to improve the profitability of our home book of about 4 points, about 2 points unfavorable from our pay per-mile car product, and about 2 points favorable from the rest of the book. We expect ADR to normalize and resume improvement over the coming quarters."

As mentioned, this was largely management-driven as the company tightened its underwriting standards and rejected renewals of home policies that it deemed risky. The result has been the improvement in gross loss ratios that we're seeing, and so far new business and cross-sales into products like Lemonade Car have helped to blunt the impact of weaker renewals. If new policy growth slows, however, we could start to see a deeper impact from weaker renewals.

The second is the potential impact from tariffs, particularly the 25% levy on auto parts. The company notes that if these rates remain in place, Lemonade would see a "single digit percentage point" impact on gross loss ratios in the Auto book alone, impacting vehicle replacement costs.

That being said, I see far more positives than negatives for Lemonade as it scales toward its goal of breakeven adjusted EBITDA by FY27. Its accelerating IFP growth is a testament to the growing popularity of its easy-to-use insurance platform, particularly among the younger generations as they enter prime home and car-buying years. Stay long here.

data.title: Northern Trust: Well-Positioned To Withstand Stagflation (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Northern Trust (NASDAQ:NTRS) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining 16%, though they are more than 10% below their highs. Shares have recovered all of their “Liberation Day” losses, which makes sense given a business model with limited tariff exposure. While shares have been a meaningful long-term underperformer, Northern Trust has outperformed since I last covered shares in October 2023 when I rated it a sell. After the pressures from the Silicon Valley Bank failure, we have seen net interest and deposit trends improve, while buoyant markets have lifted fee revenue. With significant macro uncertainties, now is a good time to revisit NTRS. I see shares around fair value.

In the company’s first quarter, reported back on April 22nd, Northern Trust earned $1.90, beating consensus by $0.06 as revenue jumped by 18% to $1.95 billion. I would note that last year, Northern Trust took $189 million of losses on its securities portfolio. That represents just over 10% of the revenue growth. Otherwise, growth would be mid-single digits, still an encouraging outcome. The business has benefited from extremely strong capital and strong pricing power over its institutional deposit base.

Northern Trust is a custody bank in an oligopoly dominated by it, State Street (STT), and Bank of New York Mellon (BK). Unlike most commercial banks, these banks have little retail deposit presence and balance sheets that generally hold cash and securities rather than having significant loan portfolios. Instead, they are key members of the “plumbing” of the financial system by maintaining custody (or holding) the assets of investment funds, institutions, etc.

This is a relatively low-fee and low-risk business. It now has $16.9 trillion of assets under custody/administration, up 3% from last year. That means its revenue was just about ~4bps on its assets. Given low fee rates, it is essential to have a significant scale, which provides a strong moat around the three players. Ultimately, Northern Trust’s growth in assets under custody will mostly be tied to market levels, as market share movements are minimal.

NTRS earns revenue in two ways, primarily: by collecting fees on the assets it oversees, and by earning interest spreads on its deposits. Asset servicing fees rose by 5% from last year to $672 million, which was 2% faster than asset growth. This business has faced ongoing fee pressure, and so the faster revenue growth is a very welcome development. We are seeing some momentum in more value-added services. Most notably, securities lending is up to 23% of revenue. Now, it only has $191 billion in assets lent out, which is only about 1.5% of its assets.

In other words, securities lending drives 15x as much revenue per dollar of assets as the total business. AUM was up 17% from last year, which drove 9% more revenue. Revenue growth lagged AUM growth, suggesting NTRS earned lower fees to grow the business. I still view this as a winning action because increasing growth at the highest-margin segment is accretive to the total business, even if its standalone pricing becomes a bit less attractive.

Additionally, NTRS has an investment management operation, highlighted by its strong wealth management presence. Wealth management AUM rose by 6% to $447 billion, while assets under custody rose by 2% to $1.1 trillion. Wealth management fees rose by 8% to $542 million. They were down 1% sequentially. Again, I was encouraged to see revenue rise more quickly than overall AUM. Revenue here will be tied to market levels, which is a reason why Q1 was a bit lower than Q4.

Alongside this fee revenue growth, noninterest expense was up 4% to $1.42 billion. This would have been up 5%, excluding the impact of the FDIC special assessment last year. This is a fairly elevated pace of spending growth, as NTRS invests in upgrading technology and enhancing offerings. Still, after this year, I would like to see expense growth slow to match some of the efficiency efforts at peers like BNY.

Because of its current level of spending, NTRS is not enjoying much bottom-line improvement in its core fee business, instead making everything on net interest income. In Q1, it generated $521 million of pre-tax income, meaning it lost $52 million outside of its net interest income. Last year, it similarly lost about $55 million, adjusting for its securities loss. BNY is now able to turn a modest profit outside of net interest income (NII), which is where I would like to see NTRS get. That is unlikely to occur before 2027, in my view.

Turning to net interest trends, I am very encouraged by its ability to reprice deposits without disrupting flows. Its net interest margin ((NIM)) rose by 8bps from last year to 1.69%, down just 2bps sequentially. Given Fed rate cuts lowered FFE by about 30bps on average, less than 7% of this flowed through to NTRS’s margin. As explained on the earnings call, Northern Trust has achieved an 86% deposit beta this cycle, vs. 72% during the tightening. It is bringing rates back down on deposits more aggressively than it raised them. Its ability to isolate out institutional deposits, where it has a near-100% beta, is leading to this ability to recapture margin.

Now, if we did see the Fed funds rate drop below 250–300bps, I expect deposit betas would slow a bit, leading to some NII pressure. However, in this environment, that seems unlikely to occur, especially with inflation risk rising amid tariffs. That should support ongoing growth in NII. In Q1, net interest income was up 7% to $574 million. This was due to NIM expansion and the fact that average earning assets rose by 3% to $138 billion.

At the same time, its portfolio is short-dated with over 25% in cash, which reduces mark-to-market risks if rates surge. Similarly, if we see an economic downturn, its portfolio has limited exposure. NTRS has just $41 billion in loans, down $600 million from last year. These are largely high-quality loans to institutional borrowers with limited credit risk. As such, it took just a $1 million provision for credit losses. NTRS has $207 million of credit reserves, meaning allowances are 2.3x nonaccrual loans. This is a strong level that should limit the need to add reserves, even in a downturn.

Northern Trust has $109 billion of interest-bearing deposits, up 11% from last year. We do continue to see a negative mix shift, with $22 billion of noninterest-bearing deposits, down 14% from last year. Just 15% of deposits are NIB, and I expect balances to be fairly flat from current levels.

Northern Trust has an excellent balance sheet. It has a 12.9% common equity tier 1 capital (CET1) ratio, up 50bps sequentially and 150bps from last year. As a result, it did $288 million of share buybacks, and its share count is down 5% from last year. It also pays a secure 3% dividend yield. NTRS targets an 11-12% capital ratio. With so much excess capital, it should have a ~100% payout ratio this year and likely in 2026 as well.

Alongside results, NTRS updated guidance. Expense growth should still be below 5% this year, which remains elevated in my view. Thanks to strong deposit growth and pricing power, management now sees middle single-digit NII growth, up from low single-digit previously, assuming stable NIB balances. Given my view that the Fed is unlikely to cut rates more than two times, I view this guidance as credible.

Given its lack of credit exposure and gearing to short-term interest rates, Northern Trust is relatively well-positioned for a stagflationary environment. With limited tariff exposure, I see about $8-$8.25 in earnings power. It should be able to return all of this capital to shareholders. I view an ~8% capital return yield as fair value, given its low margins and limited medium-term growth. As such, at $100, I view the stock as essentially at fair value. If we saw a pullback towards $90, I would buy shares. Similarly, if we saw it return to the highs, I would sell. But for now, NTRS is likely to be a market performer and a relative safe haven amid macro uncertainty.

data.title: Cisco, Walmart Set To Report Results As Markets Watch Out For Core CPI
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's major averages pushed higher on Thursday after President Donald Trump announced a new trade deal with the United Kingdom. The agreement will provide increased access for billions of dollars of U.S. products, and eliminate numerous non-tariff barriers. "Markets only really care how far and how fast Trump retreats from tariffs", UBS' Paul Donovan said. The Fed, on Wednesday, kept its benchmark rate at 4.25%-4.50% for the third straight meeting, as widely expected, even as it sees the risks of inflation and unemployment rising. Equities saw a muted response to the Fed decision, but the S&P 500 managed to post a gain by the close thanks to a late rally following a report that the Trump administration is planning to rescind Biden-era AI chip curbs.

Investors will keep a close eye on economic events scheduled for next week, such as, NFIB Small Business Optimism Index, and Core CPI on Tuesday. Tuesday will also see the release of the UK's Unemployment Rate, and Japan's PPI. Meanwhile, Thursday will see the release of UK's first quarter GDP, and United States' Jobless Claims, and Core PPI.

Walmart (WMT), Intuitive Machines (LUNR), Cisco (CSCO), Boot Barn Holdings (BOOT), and Hertz Global (HTZ) will report earnings next week.

______________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, May 12: Fox Corp (FOXA), Acadia Healthcare (ACHC), Hertz Global (HTZ). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, May 13: JD.com (JD), Intuitive Machines. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, May 14: Cisco, Nextracker (NXT), Boot Barn Holdings. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, May 15: Walmart, Alibaba ADR (BABA). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: WW International (WW) and Wolfspeed (WOLF) have a higher level of implied volatility than normal based on options trading. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include NewHold Investment Corp. III (NHIC), Asset Entities (ASST), and Regulus Therapeutics (RGLS). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Compass Diversified (CODI), Myriad Genetics (MYGN), and Prothena (PRTA).

IPO watch: The lockup periods for Thumzup Media (TZUP), Gelteq (GELS), and Polyrizon are set to expire on Monday. While, Yuanbao (YB) is expected to price its IPO on Monday and begin trading on Tuesday. OMS Energy Technologies (OMSE) is also expected to begin trading on Tuesday.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include Apple (AAPL), KKR & Co (KKR), Ford Motor (F), Visa (V), Delta Air Lines (DAL), Kenvue (KVUE), Target (TGT), Microsoft (MSFT), and Eli Lilly (LLY). Companies forecast to increase their quarterly dividends include Advanced Drainage (WMS) from $0.16 to $0.17, Everest Group (EG) from $2 to $2.10, Cutiss-Wright (CW) from $0.21 to $0.22, Chubb (CB) from $0.91 to $0.95, HNI (HNI) from $0.33 to $0.34, and Ingredion (INGR) from $0.80 to $0.82. Read through some dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

Investor Events: The three-day Consensus 2025 event will begin on Wednesday in Toronto. The crypto conference hosted by Coindesk is one of the most anticipated of the year. The Sohn Investment Conference will also begin on Wednesday in New York City. Meanwhile, the Oppenheimer Israeli Conference will begin on Friday and will see participation from Pagaya Technologies (PGY) and Perion Network (PERI).

Cisco Earnings Preview: Cisco is scheduled to announce its third quarter earnings next week, with analysts expecting earnings per share of $0.92, on revenue of $14.05B, a 10% increase from last year. UBS expects Cisco to report revenue upside, driven by both core networking demand and Splunk. UBS, however, remains focused on potential headwinds from efficiency efforts at the US Federal government, but notes Cisco's exposure is manageable at only ~10% of rev. Regarding tariffs, the Bank says, levies should not materially impact the April quarter and most of the July quarter given the 90-day pause.

Walmart Earnings Preview: Walmart is set to announce its first quarter earnings next week. Analysts expect the retail giant to report a 3.3% decline in EPS to $0.58, on a revenue of $164.5B. Oppenheimer expects Walmart's EPS to fall below Street expectations, and expects management to likely reaffirm full-year guidance. The retailer recently noted a wider range of outcomes around its Q1 operating income, driven by less favorable category mix, additional expenses, and opportunities to invest in price.

data.title: Treasury Tribulations, And The Bessent Put
data.description:
Summary

After going through the longest yield curve inversion period in history, the recent macroeconomic trends surrounding U.S. Treasuries make for a complex investing landscape, requiring multi-asset class allocation. Specifically, the U.S. Treasury Secretary is advocating for the Trump Administration’s three-pronged economic plan, consisting of trade, tax cuts, and deregulation. While the Secretary’s commentary has provided relative calm to the bond markets, the next six-to-twelve months bring with it new challenges: tariffs that could devalue the U.S. dollar, shrinking GDP that risks a recession, and tax cuts that pose the threat of inflation. From an investor’s standpoint, one can find respite in gold, treasuries, and – for those with a greater risk appetite – America-first equities.

Treasury Issuance Data

Last year, I wrote a short primer on Treasuries, leading to possible investments in a bond ladder, precious metals, or bond ETFs. Now that investors have been through the longest period of yield curve inversion in history, this might be a good time to revisit the macroeconomic trends across the landscape. Uncertainty is the name of the game, and we’ll explore a few ways to play it.

The following table represents an update since April 2024 for gross issuance of treasury debt, net issuance in treasury debt, both in billions of dollars, as well as yields on 6 month, 5 year, and 30 year treasuries, since 2013. Note that yields are marked as of the beginning of the calendar year.

We can draw the following conclusions: Gross issuance of T-Bills is still staggering – the highest ever – as the Treasury Department continued to fund operations via short term debt. Net issuance of +$1.8 trillion in 2024 backs up the thesis that post-pandemic, the United States government borrowed heavily in a partial attempt to monetize the debt while also funding…well…whatever it is the Federal Government does with money. Last year I noted a particular disdain for issuing T-bills while the yield curve was inverted. One could infer that the Biden Administration was waiting for interest rates to come down. Yet, regardless of the rationale, this strategy ended up costing Americans more money than was necessary:

“...governments do not always behave in the best interest of its people. To prove the point, we need only look at the present day. We have had the most net issuance of T-bills in recent history, at the highest possible interest rate. Who benefits?”

In a recent interview, former Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen appeared on CNBC’s “Squawk Box”, and she was asked a question by Kelly Evans regarding this exact issue. Yellen was asked if it is a good idea to issue T-Bills as opposed to longer duration notes & bonds – a behavior she engaged in as Treasury Secretary. I’m paraphrasing her response, in which she stated that the Treasury follows a strategy of “regular, predictable, orderly issuance”, and that there was no plan to play the market (when yields were inverted). Her rationale for excessive T-Bill issuance was that due to the debt ceiling reaching critical mass in October 2023, the Treasury Account was near zero, and she needed to refill the Government’s coffers…her commentary explains why Treasury issued $1.9 trillion in T-Bills over 2023 (see the net issuance table above).

A quick look at the Treasury Account, Opening Balance, is shown below (since 2022), and does, in fact, correlate with Former Secretary Yellen’s comments. The account was at its lowest circa May 2023, at about $37 billion:

Why the Treasury Account fell so low remains a conversation for another time. The real question, then, is whether or not Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent will go down the same path, and issue T-Bills to fund the government in the short term. Indeed, there are already inklings of an August debt ceiling crisis, should Congress fail to raise the debt ceiling yet again.

The Bessent Put

Secretary Bessent has taken a different approach than Janet Yellen, and he has been out there making his case. We’re no longer talking about the Fed put, and I’ll go so far as to say that Secretary Bessent is providing markets with the calming force of a “put”, as President Trump continues to move forward with tariffs across the globe. Given some of his public statements, the Treasury Secretary is advocating policy in direct opposition to his predecessor’s methods referenced above. To demonstrate, consider Bessent’s remarks at the Milken Institute early this month, in which he praised the President’s approach, and highlighted the three-pronged strategy of trade, tax cuts, and deregulation. In a response to a question about the volatility in the 10-year, Bessent refuted the “regular, predictable, orderly issuance” paradigm:

“…I think a lot of what we saw in April was this periodic unwinding that I’ve experienced, you’ve experienced, most people in this room have experienced in their career. I’m a big believer in the - in the Hyman Minsky, stability leads to instability. So I think we probably had a very stable period. A lot of leverage players built up very large positions. There was an uncertainty shock. They’re out. But what we’re trying to do is create, go back to the Trump 1.0 non-inflationary growth. So I think if we can put non-inflationary growth in place and take away […] the credit risk of the US government, then I think rates will naturally come down. […] I call it reprivatizing the government, or reprivatizing the US economy…”

Bessent goes on to explain deficit reductions and reducing the size of government as ways to address the debt problem. In essence, where the Biden Administration opted for treasury stability (re: Yellen’s remarks) and government spending (for example, the Inflation Reduction Act), the Trump Administration seeks to cut government spending, make trade deals, and lower taxes.

What’s the Point? Or Rather, How Do We Play the Hand?

I invite you to read through the tea leaves in regard to Bessent’s commentary. The Trump administration is going to either implement tariffs or sign trade deals. They have already worked to reduce the deficit via DOGE and other executive actions. Finally, they will attempt to push through a tax deal with Congress later this year. The implications of such actions are starting to be revealed, leading to three pertinent questions. Could the U.S. dollar lose value relative to other currencies? Could a recession occur by way of high unemployment (i.e., cutting government jobs)? Will lower tax rates generate growth? Let’s take each of these in turn.

First, the basis trade that sparked a bond rout had investors worried about large treasury holders like Japan and China, even prompting ideas that China is retaliating against the U.S. by unloading their treasury holdings. More than likely, however, it seems to be largely an unwinding across funds and institutions; moreover, Japan’s finance minister indicated the country will not sell Treasuries as a trade war action. That leaves currency risk, in that traders will head for the Yen (or anything but the dollar) as a safe haven asset. If so, and the dollar continues to lose value, it would be a positive for precious metals like gold. My favorite gold trade over the past year has been Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), and it looks like there is more room to run. Other alternatives include miners like Newmont Corporation (NEM) & Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM), as well as ETFs like SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) and iShares Gold Trust (IAU). Agnico has performed well; nevertheless, I prefer Wheaton due to the streaming nature of the business, as it doesn’t incur mining expenses. Furthermore, an added incentive for gold buyers stems from economic problems in China. The country is going through a real estate crash, lower interest rates, and has banned bitcoin purchases, leaving its citizens with fewer alternatives in a downtrodden market: gold is the prevailing asset.

Second, the latest GDP figure indicated a contraction in Q1, leading to increased predictions for a recession amidst job losses and a high trade deficit. Two facets subtracted from GDP: companies who stocked up on pre-tariff inventory, and government cuts that left ~130,000 without a job. Notably, at 4.2% the unemployment rate has stayed relatively steady over the past year, while non-farm payrolls have ticked upward. These facts present a dichotomy between shrinking the budget deficit and as Bessent states, “reprivatizing the U.S. government”. On the one hand, cutting government spending negatively affects GDP. On the other hand, higher job growth minimizes the hit to rising unemployment. The net effect could be the “loosening” of the job market and the consequent upshot will be that the Federal Reserve has an opening to cut rates.

Third, the pending budget reconciliation & voting on Trump’s tax cuts promises to bring division among all interested parties (politicians & investors included). Secretary Bessent – in line with Trump’s vision – believes tax cuts are a spoke in the flywheel of long-term economic growth. Yet, many budgetary prognosticators view the tax cuts as antithetical to a balanced budget. Consider an article by Erica York, Garrett Watson, William McBride, and Alex Muresianu of the Tax Foundation, in which they state that Trump’s tax cuts – assuming passage through the House and Senate – would “decrease federal tax revenue by $4.5 trillion from 2025 through 2034”. Similarly, GDP would increase by 1.1%, but would only offset about $710 billion of the assumed revenue losses. In their eyes, it is a $5.3 trillion “deficit-financed tax cut”, with the inference being that the Treasury would ultimately have to issue more debt in order to pay for the tax cut, and we know by now that increasing the money supply results in higher inflation.

The tax cuts are – in my opinion – the most misunderstood part of the economic plan. While the Tax Foundation piece highlights revenue shortfalls, history has told us that cuts in the tax rate actually lead to increased revenue for the federal government. This is a stance Dr. Thomas Sowell has argued for multiple times, including in a 2012 interview discussing his essay, “Trickle Down Theory and Tax Cuts for the Rich”. Beginning at minute 12:50, we get a synopsis of four different times in history where cuts in the tax rate have led to higher tax revenues: Andrew Mellon’s tax cuts in the 1920s, JFK’s tax cuts in the 1960s, Reagan’s supply side economics in the 1980s, and George W. Bush’s cuts in the early 2000s. Dr. Sowell explains the effect on the economy in these cases:

“The effect on the economy was to increase the rate of growth, increase the revenue received by the government, the rich not only paid more taxes after the ‘tax cuts for the rich’ as they call it, they paid a higher percentage of all taxes.”

I dug a little bit deeper into the 2017 TCJA, and it turns out that from 2018 to 2020 (Trump 1.0), tax revenues increased each year, from $3.32 trillion (2017) to $3.42 trillion (2020), while the tax-to-GDP ratio decreased from 11.5% (2017) to 9.9%, 9.9% and 10.2% (2018-2020, respectively). Dr. Sowell’s reasoning appears validated, and we can now highlight two modes of thought: either lower tax rates will lead to a higher deficit & persistent inflation, or lower tax rates will lead to higher revenues & accelerating growth.

Interest Rates, Bonds, and Stocks

All told, the budget reconciliation, the necessity for a debt ceiling deal, and the end of the 90-day tariff pause all add to the uncertainty facing investors this summer. We’ve discussed previously the opportunity for investing in precious metals – namely gold – to circumvent the risk of treasury sell-offs and China-based recession. In terms of an American recession or the impact of tax cuts, we want to try and make things as easy as possible. Along the same lines, Treasury Secretary Bessent is providing us with the necessary clues. His goal is to “take away the credit risk of the U.S. government”. In layman’s terms, he wants interest rates lower. If interest rates are going down, then we can buy bonds.

Since the yield curve inversion is over, and the Fed sets short-term rates, it makes sense to look out toward longer duration. One option is the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF). It has lagged the broader market, dropping 22% over the past five years. But as interest rates fall, we can expect IEF to gain ground, while also providing a 3.7% yield. A simple bond calculator will tell you that an 8-year duration bond trading at $94.75 (IEF’s NAV), yielding 3.7%, will trade at ~$110/share when interest rates are at 2%. Of course, this is a rough calculation, and I’m assuming the Fed will drop interest rates over the next two to three years. Nevertheless, bonds are the traditional route for safe haven assets. On a similar note, Alpha Architect 1-3 Month Box ETF (BOXX) presents an interesting way to “hold cash”. BOXX essentially uses SPX options to generate income that mimics short-term yields. It is a terrific way to allocate cash in a tax efficient manner. However, keep in mind that should the Fed lower rates, in theory BOXX will yield lower returns.

Finally, if – like me – you subscribe to the idea of lower tax rates generating more tax revenues, then the shrinking deficit will coincide with more fruitful economic growth, and equities will be one of the primary beneficiaries. In this regard, we should look for so-called America-first businesses, with favorable valuation metrics, and take a long-term horizon. In particular, CF Industries (CF) fits the bill, along with BlueLinx Holdings (BXC) and Viper Energy (VNOM). Qualitatively speaking, each of these businesses are America-centric, and each carries with it their own style of moat. Whether it is unique location (CF), advantageous logistics (BlueLinx), or a strong royalty business model (Viper), these companies should be able to navigate market uncertainty and continue to grow over the long term.

Conclusion, Risks, and Other Considerations

To sum up, we’ve discussed gold, bonds, equities, and cash alternatives as viable investments in the current environment. Perhaps the biggest risk to the investor is allocating too heavily toward one asset class over another. Indeed, it is true that the Trump Administration must follow through with trade deals, tax cuts, and deregulation in order to avoid economic calamity. Treasury Secretary Bessent – in tranquil fashion – has taken a different approach than his predecessor, so much so that he has managed to instill a bit of calm in the bond markets. On the other hand, the “Bessent Put” can only go so far, and Trump’s 90-day pause on tariffs will soon come to an end. If tariffs lead to a devaluation of the U.S. dollar, gold will continue to dominate. But there are other cogs in the wheel that need to be satisfied: deficit reduction and tax cuts.

The Federal Reserve is going to walk a tight rope between inflation and unemployment, and given how big the federal government is, improving the budget deficit will lean the Fed toward interest rate cuts. One way to make sense of it all from an investment perspective is to simply buy treasuries. They are a safe haven asset, and will appreciate during the downward rate cycle. The last piece of the puzzle – tax cuts – requires a bit more understanding of history in order to appreciate the different scenarios we could face in the next six to twelve months. Some believe we are headed for stagflation and economic disaster. But the reality may not be so bleak. If tax cuts lead to tax revenue growth, then we are at a particular buying opportunity for companies with good long-term prospects. Given that uncertainty is the name of the game, we can still play it with precious metals, bonds, stocks, and cash opportunities.

data.title: Sturm, Ruger & Company: Investing Through The Long Term Has Proven Unprofitable
data.description:
Today I want to take a look at one of the most American companies there is, the Sturm, Ruger & Company (RGR), which, as you probably know, designs, manufactures, and sells firearms in the US. Since its last earnings report on the 30th of April, RGR is down by 18.03% after missing big both on their earnings and revenue.

Before diving into the report and the company's fundamentals, I want to explain why I rate them as a HOLD, and wouldn't advise investing in them if you plan to hold them through the long term. My thesis is that as leisure gun sales don't have a reason to grow at a decent rate through the long term, the idea that the company would experience steady growth and bring competitive returns to its investors is far-fetched.

Nevertheless, before stating the reasons for my belief and digging into the company's financials, let's look at the most recent Q1 2025 earnings report.

Analysis

in their Q1 '25 earnings report, RGR reported EPS of $0.46, felt really short of the forecasted $0.67, specifically by $0.21 or 31.34%. The revenue was also reported as a miss by about $13.00M, expected at $148.60M but reported at $135.70M.

Now, despite this miss, RGR remains debt-free, which is something I love and find really important, especially for a not so steadily growing company such as themselves. Something I want to establish before diving into the full analysis is that I find RGR a good, well-managed business, but from an investor's perspective, I wouldn't choose it as a long-term investment due to the risk-to-return ratio being too high.

Revenue Growth

When we look at the company's 5Y revenue, we will see that it has fallen by 5.84% since 2020, falling from $569.00M to $536.00M in 2024 at a CAGR of -0.09%.

Of course, one of the main reasons for this stagnating/falling top line is the elevated inflation the US has been facing for the past few years, which has prompted many consumers to cut on spending, including the spending on new firearms. This means that the falling 5Y revenue cannot be used to define the overall direction of the company.

But looking at the 10Y revenue growth, we will find that it has as well been stagnating, moving from $551.00M in 2020 to the current $536.00M, meaning that when accounting for an average long-term inflation of 2.24%, we will find that RGR has been losing revenue at a CAGR of 2.93% through the past 10Y.

The bottom line isn't much better

Of course, the value of a company isn't either well reflected off of the revenue, but rather through net income. Nevertheless, the bottom line hasn't been doing too well either, falling even harder than the revenue through the past 5Y, specifically by 6.19% since 2020, at a CAGR of 10.13%.

Through the longer term (10Y) it is also down by 49.75%, falling from $62.10M in 2015 to $31.00M in 2024, accounting for a CAGR of -8.09% and a CAGR of -10.33% accounting for inflation.

Great Underperformance

I believe that both the net income and revenue falling prove my point that the company isn't the best one to hold through the long term. The movement of the stock price also fully supports my long-term sentiment, as through the past 10 years the share price has decreased by 40.15%, and through the past 30 years, the company is up by only 137.00%, greatly underperforming the market.

This stock price underperformance would be understandable if RGR had a high, steady dividend, which they don't. Accounting for its dividends, (the total return), RGR has returned 761.04% through the past 30 years, about 1000.00% less than the return of the SPY500.

Dividend safety and growth

Talking about the dividend, through the past 5 years, the yearly dividend amount paid out including special dividends has decreased by 65.68%, moving at a negative CAGR of 13.04%. Despite decreasing this dividend, the 5Y FCF payout ratio is sitting at 51.87% and has been really volatile, moving up and down according to the company's FCF, which is down by 62.00% through the same time period. This means that even though being debt-free, RGR doesn't have much maneuverability regarding the dividends due to their unpredictable FCF growth.

Again, this is one of the reasons why I would advise long-term investors to stay away from the company. Their current dividend yield is sitting at only 2.10%, much lower than the yield on the 10-year US Treasury notes, held at around 4.31%. Meaning that with little upside potential from the stock itself, investing in the US Treasury proves to be a much more viable option, taking into account the risk-to-return ratio.

Future Outlook

Based on the historical 20-year growth of US gun sales, growing from about 0.60M sales per month in 2000 to 1.00M sales in 2020, at a CAGR of 2.58%, we can deduce that into the future, RGR doesn't have much potential to change their stagnating top and bottom line, meaning that their stock price growth will probably keep underperforming the market, making them a bad option for a long-term investment.

Valuation

Despite the company not having a too bright of a future, the reason why I rate them as a HOLD and not a SELL is because of their fair valuation.

Looking at the DCF Model and predicting a low future 10Y + FCF CAGR of 3.70% and a discount rate of 6.67%, higher than the WACC of 4.36%, we will arrive at an intrinsic value of $41.76, quite higher than the current trading price of $33.33.

The main reason for such a high valuation is the company's very low total debt, sitting at only $2.22M, allowing RGR to return most of their FCF to investors through share buybacks and dividends in the coming years.

For the second valuation model, I want to take a look at the Graham's Formula, where we calculate the intrinsic value based on the future 5Y EPS growth. Into the next 2 years, analysts predict the EPS to grow at a CAGR of around 12.50%, so combining this with my own sentiment based on the historical growth, I will predict the 5Y EPS CAGR at 8.33%.

This growth is higher than the average 10Y or 5Y CAGR due to my belief that the post-Covid-19 inflation won't last forever, meaning that within the next few years, consumers will go back to spending more on leisure firearms, boosting the company's bottom line from the pit inflation has pushed them into, until it comes back to normalized levels.

Nevertheless, combining this 5Y CAGR with the current EPS of $1.83, we will get an intrinsic value of $32.71, only 1.86% lower than the current trading price of $33.33.

Now, for the last model, I want to compare RGR to probably its biggest competitor, Smith & Wesson Brands, in the Multiples Valuation. SWBI currently has a PE ratio of 14.08, which is quite lower than the PE of RGR sitting at 18.21, meaning that when we multiply the EPS of RGR sitting at $1.83 with the PE of SWBI, we will get an intrinsic value of $25.76, lower than the current trading price, showing that the company is possibly overvalued compared to its competition.

Nevertheless, averaging out the 3 values we have calculated, we will get an ultimate intrinsic value of $33.13, only 0.60% lower than the current trading price of $33.33. Though with a safety margin of 10.00%, our acceptable buying price would be $29.82, supporting my HOLD rating.

Conclusion

To conclude this article, the reason why I rate Sturm Ruger & Company as a HOLD and not as a SELL is because at its current valuation and price, there is a possibility that through the short term, as inflation weakens (a few years) the stock price will grow, therefore selling now, whether your basis cost is lower or higher than the current trading price wouldn't make too much sense.

The message I tried to push in this article is that through the long term, holding the company has proven unprofitable compared to the overall market, which offers much less risk. Therefore, with low change in the demand for leisure firearms in the US, I trust that in the future the stock has low chances to beat history, meaning that a long-term investment accounting for the risk-to-return ratio would be unwise.

Nevertheless, I hope that you found the article helpful, and let me know in the comments if you agree with the thesis.
